stem:
A school librarian reported that students are checking out more nonfiction books than ever before. She pointed to data collected over the past year to support the claim.

**Books Checked Out at Lincoln Middle School Library (2024)**

| Genre | Spring | Fall |
|---|---|---|
| Fiction | 420 | 410 |
| Nonfiction | 180 | 340 |
| Poetry | 30 | 35 |

Which choice best uses data from the table to support the librarian's claim?

choices.text: Nonfiction checkouts climbed from 180 in spring to 340 in fall.
Fiction remains the most popular genre at the library.
Poetry checkouts rose from 30 in spring to 35 in fall.
Students checked out books in every genre across both seasons.

explanation: The librarian's claim is about nonfiction checkouts rising. Only choice A cites the specific nonfiction jump (180 to 340) from the table that directly supports this. Choice B is about fiction, C is about a tiny poetry change that doesn't speak to nonfiction, and D is too vague to support any specific trend.

stem:
A city council member argued that the new bike lanes on Main Street have made commuting by bicycle more popular. She cited traffic counts taken before and after the lanes were installed.

**Weekday Bicycle Counts on Main Street**

| Year | Morning (7–9 AM) | Evening (4–6 PM) |
|---|---|---|
| 2022 (before lanes) | 42 | 55 |
| 2024 (after lanes) | 118 | 140 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the council member's claim?

choices.text: Morning bicycle counts increased from 42 in 2022 to 118 in 2024.
Evening counts were always higher than morning counts.
In 2024, fewer than 150 bicycles passed during the evening rush.
The counts were taken during weekday rush hours in two different years.

explanation: Only choice A pulls a before/after pair from the table (42 to 118) that directly shows bicycle commuting grew after the lanes were installed. Choice B compares morning to evening in a way that ignores the before/after change, C states a single 2024 figure without comparison, and D just describes the methodology.

stem:
A student journalist wrote that the cafeteria's new salad bar is popular with students. She included a table of daily lunch purchases to back up her claim.

**Westview High Cafeteria Purchases (first week of salad bar)**

| Day | Hot lunch | Sandwich | Salad bar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monday | 310 | 140 | 95 |
| Wednesday | 290 | 135 | 160 |
| Friday | 285 | 130 | 215 |

Which choice most effectively uses the data to support the journalist's claim?

choices.text: Hot lunch sales dropped from 310 on Monday to 285 on Friday.
Salad bar purchases rose from 95 on Monday to 215 on Friday.
Sandwich sales stayed between 130 and 140 across the three days.
The cafeteria served students three types of meals during the week.

explanation: The claim is about the salad bar's popularity, and only choice B directly cites its climb from 95 to 215 — the data point that shows growing student interest in it. Choices A and C describe other menu items and don't speak to the salad bar, and D is a generic statement with no numbers.

stem:
A park ranger claimed that visitors to Pine Ridge State Park prefer hiking trails over fishing spots. He pointed to a recent visitor survey.

**Pine Ridge State Park Visitor Activities (June 2024, n = 500)**

| Activity | Visitors |
|---|---|
| Hiking | 265 |
| Picnicking | 110 |
| Fishing | 85 |
| Camping | 40 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the ranger's claim?

choices.text: Camping drew only 40 visitors, the smallest group in the survey.
Picnicking was more popular than fishing among visitors.
265 of 500 surveyed visitors reported hiking, compared to 85 who reported fishing.
A total of 500 visitors were surveyed in June 2024.

explanation: The ranger's claim compares hiking to fishing specifically, and only choice C pulls both of those numbers from the table (265 vs. 85) to make that direct comparison. Choice A is about camping, B compares picnicking and fishing (not hiking), and D only cites the total sample size.

stem:
A biology teacher wrote that the monarch butterflies her class raised had a high survival rate compared to those in the wild. She shared the class's data.

**Ms. Patel's Monarch Rearing Project**

| Stage | Started | Survived |
|---|---|---|
| Caterpillar → Chrysalis | 50 | 47 |
| Chrysalis → Adult | 47 | 44 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the teacher's claim?

choices.text: The class started with 50 caterpillars at the beginning of the project.
Three caterpillars died before reaching the chrysalis stage.
Of the 50 caterpillars the class started with, 44 survived to become adult butterflies.
Some monarchs survived while others did not during the project.

explanation: A survival rate claim needs both a start count and an end count. Only choice C cites both the starting count (50) and the final survivors (44) — the data pair that lets a reader compute the high rate (44/50 = 88%). The other options name only one figure or make no quantitative comparison.

stem:
A school nurse argued that the district's new hand-washing campaign is reducing the number of students sent home sick. She pulled data from the health office log.

**Students Sent Home Sick — Jefferson Elementary**

| Month | Before campaign (2023) | After campaign (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| October | 58 | 22 |
| November | 64 | 28 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the nurse's claim?

choices.text: In October, the number of students sent home dropped from 58 in 2023 to 22 in 2024.
Students were still being sent home sick even after the campaign began.
November counts were higher than October counts in both years.
The campaign happened between the 2023 and 2024 school years.

explanation: Only choice A uses a before/after pair from the table (58 down to 22) to show the reduction the nurse is claiming. Choice B actually undercuts the claim, C compares months rather than years, and D is purely background, not evidence.

stem:
A coffee-shop owner claimed that her new oat-milk lattes have become the most ordered drink on her menu. She checked the register's weekly totals.

**Drinks Sold — Brew Street (week of March 3)**

| Drink | Orders |
|---|---|
| Regular latte | 212 |
| Oat-milk latte | 298 |
| Drip coffee | 175 |
| Espresso | 90 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the owner's claim?

choices.text: Regular lattes were the second-most popular drink that week.
Espresso drinks were ordered only 90 times during the week.
With 298 orders, oat-milk lattes outsold every other drink on the menu.
Brew Street sells four kinds of drinks on its main menu.

explanation: The claim is that oat-milk lattes are now the #1 seller. Only choice C cites the specific oat-milk number (298) and frames it as beating every other drink — the exact comparison the claim requires. A and B name other drinks, and D just counts menu categories.

stem:
A scout leader reported that the troop's recycling drive collected more aluminum cans than plastic bottles. He recorded the weekend's totals.

**Troop 42 Recycling Drive — Saturday**

| Item | Count |
|---|---|
| Aluminum cans | 640 |
| Plastic bottles | 395 |
| Glass bottles | 120 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the scout leader's claim?

choices.text: Glass bottles were the least-collected item at 120.
The troop collected 640 aluminum cans compared to 395 plastic bottles.
The drive took place on a Saturday with many volunteers.
Hundreds of recyclable items were collected during the drive.

explanation: Only choice B cites both numbers the claim compares (640 cans vs. 395 bottles) directly from the table, which is exactly the side-by-side evidence needed. A is about glass bottles, C describes logistics, and D is vague.

stem:
A researcher studying urban gardens argued that the amount of produce a community garden yields depends heavily on the number of weekly volunteer hours, not the total plot size. She compiled the following data from five gardens.

**Community Garden Yields — 2024 Growing Season**

| Garden | Plot size (m²) | Weekly volunteer hours | Produce (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maple | 120 | 6 | 180 |
| Birch | 200 | 8 | 260 |
| Cedar | 180 | 18 | 620 |
| Willow | 260 | 20 | 690 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's claim?

choices.text: Maple and Birch together grew 440 kilograms of produce last season.
The largest garden, Willow, produced the greatest total yield of 690 kilograms.
Smaller gardens tended to produce smaller yields than larger ones.
Cedar, a mid-sized plot, produced 620 kilograms with 18 volunteer hours per week, while similarly sized Birch produced just 260 kilograms with only 8 volunteer hours.

explanation: The researcher's claim isolates volunteer hours as the driver, not plot size. Only choice D compares two gardens of similar size (Cedar 180 m² vs. Birch 200 m²) with very different volunteer hours (18 vs. 8) and very different yields (620 vs. 260 kg), which is exactly the evidence that separates the effect of hours from the effect of size. The other options either conflate size with yield (B, C) or give an irrelevant sum (A).

stem:
A transportation planner claimed that adding a covered bus shelter at a stop significantly increases ridership, especially on rainy days. She compared two nearby stops before and after one of them received a shelter.

**Average Daily Boardings (2024)**

| Stop | Dry-day boardings | Rainy-day boardings |
|---|---|---|
| Elm Ave (no shelter) | 95 | 62 |
| Oak St, before shelter | 100 | 65 |
| Oak St, after shelter | 108 | 140 |

Which choice most effectively uses the data from the table to support the planner's claim?

choices.text: After the shelter was added, Oak St had more rainy-day boardings than Elm Ave.
Elm Ave had only 62 rainy-day boardings in 2024.
Oak St and Elm Ave are nearby stops with comparable daily ridership.
At Oak St, rainy-day boardings rose from 65 before the shelter to 140 after it, while dry-day boardings ticked up only slightly from 100 to 108.

explanation: The claim has two parts: ridership goes up overall, and especially in the rain. Only choice D captures both — Oak St's rainy-day jump (65 → 140) plus a much smaller dry-day change (100 → 108). A is a snapshot comparison that doesn't speak to the before/after effect, B reports one number without context, and C is background only.

stem:
A conservationist argued that planting native milkweed in school gardens leads to larger increases in monarch caterpillar counts than planting non-native flowers. She tracked counts at four schools over one summer.

**Monarch Caterpillars Observed (Summer 2024)**

| School | Planting | June | August |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adams | Non-native flowers | 4 | 5 |
| Brooks | Non-native flowers | 3 | 4 |
| Carter | Native milkweed | 5 | 38 |
| Dawson | Native milkweed | 6 | 44 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the conservationist's claim?

choices.text: Dawson had more caterpillars in August than any other school in the study.
Every school in the study gained at least one caterpillar between June and August.
In June, all four schools had fewer than ten caterpillars.
Native milkweed schools saw caterpillar counts jump from 5–6 in June to 38–44 in August, while non-native flower schools barely changed, rising only from 3–4 to 4–5.

explanation: The claim is comparative: native milkweed yields a larger increase than non-native flowers. Only choice D contrasts the native-milkweed jump (5–6 → 38–44) with the flat non-native result (3–4 → 4–5), which is exactly the comparison the claim requires. A names one site, B and C describe the whole group without distinguishing the two plantings.

stem:
A sleep researcher wrote that shifting a high school's start time later in the morning raises the share of students getting at least eight hours of sleep on school nights. She compared survey results from two neighboring schools.

**Students Reporting ≥ 8 Hours of Sleep on School Nights**

| School | Start time | 2022 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|
| North HS | 7:30 AM (unchanged) | 28% | 30% |
| South HS | 7:30 → 8:30 AM (2023) | 27% | 52% |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's claim?

choices.text: After shifting its start time in 2023, South HS saw its share of students getting at least eight hours of sleep climb from 27% to 52%, while North HS, which kept its 7:30 AM start, moved only from 28% to 30%.
North HS saw its share of well-rested students rise slightly from 28% to 30%.
Both schools are located near each other and serve similar populations.
Only about half of South HS students still reported too little sleep in 2024.

explanation: Supporting the claim requires showing that the school that shifted its start later improved more than the one that didn't. Only choice A contrasts South HS's large jump (27% → 52%) after the later start with North HS's flat 28% → 30%, isolating the effect of the time change. B cites only the unchanged school, C is background, and D reframes a single data point without a comparison.

stem:
A food-service manager argued that offering fruit as the default dessert — rather than cookies — dramatically increases student fruit consumption without reducing overall dessert choices taken. He tracked a one-month pilot at two middle schools.

**Desserts Taken Per School Day (average)**

| School | Default dessert | Fruit taken | Cookie taken | Total dessert taken |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ridge MS | Cookie (control) | 35 | 190 | 225 |
| Valley MS | Fruit (pilot) | 170 | 55 | 225 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the manager's claim?

choices.text: Valley MS students averaged 170 fruits taken per day compared to 35 at Ridge MS, while both schools had the same total of 225 desserts taken per day.
At Ridge MS, cookies were taken far more often than fruit.
Both schools participated in a month-long dessert pilot study.
Valley MS students took more fruit than cookies on average.

explanation: The claim has two parts: (1) fruit consumption goes up, and (2) total desserts taken does not go down. Only choice A backs both — the big fruit increase (35 → 170) and the unchanged 225 total at both schools. B and D only speak to relative picks at one school, and C gives no data at all.

stem:
A journalist wrote that e-bike sales, not traditional bike sales, are driving the recent growth in the bicycle industry. To support her argument, she included national sales data.

**U.S. Bicycle Sales (millions of units)**

| Year | Traditional bikes | E-bikes |
|---|---|---|
| 2019 | 17.4 | 0.4 |
| 2021 | 17.9 | 0.9 |
| 2023 | 18.1 | 2.2 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the journalist's claim?

choices.text: In 2023, traditional bikes still outsold e-bikes by millions of units.
The data cover bicycle sales in the United States across three years.
From 2019 to 2023, e-bike sales rose from 0.4 million to 2.2 million units, while traditional bike sales barely moved, going from 17.4 million to 18.1 million.
E-bike sales more than doubled between 2021 and 2023.

explanation: The claim is that e-bikes, not traditional bikes, are driving growth. Only choice C contrasts the sharp e-bike growth (0.4 → 2.2 million) with nearly flat traditional-bike sales (17.4 → 18.1 million), isolating where the growth is coming from. A actually points away from the claim, B is methodology, and D mentions only e-bikes without the contrast.

stem:
An economist argued that raising the state minimum wage in 2022 did not reduce restaurant employment, contrary to what critics predicted. He tracked employment across similar counties.

**Restaurant Employment Index (2021 = 100)**

| County | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Allen (no wage hike) | 100 | 101 | 103 |
| Baker (no wage hike) | 100 | 100 | 102 |
| Clark (wage hike) | 100 | 102 | 104 |
| Douglas (wage hike) | 100 | 101 | 103 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the economist's claim?

choices.text: Allen and Baker, which did not raise wages, had slightly different 2023 employment indices.
Restaurant employment grew in every county in the table between 2021 and 2023.
Clark County saw the largest increase, with employment rising from 100 to 104.
By 2023, restaurant employment in the wage-hike counties (Clark, Douglas) reached 104 and 103, essentially matching the no-wage-hike counties (Allen, Baker), which reached 103 and 102.

explanation: The claim is that the wage hike did not reduce employment. Only choice D compares the wage-hike counties side by side with the no-wage-hike counties (104/103 vs. 103/102), showing employment in the wage-hike group held up or slightly outperformed — exactly the economist's point. A is a minor detail, B lumps all counties together without isolating the wage effect, and C names only one county.

stem:
A school's athletic director argued that the new strength-and-conditioning program reduced injuries across all sports, not just football. She shared year-over-year injury counts.

**Reported Injuries at Kennedy High**

| Sport | 2022–23 (before) | 2023–24 (after) |
|---|---|---|
| Football | 24 | 12 |
| Basketball | 14 | 7 |
| Soccer | 18 | 9 |
| Track | 10 | 5 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the athletic director's claim?

choices.text: Football injuries fell from 24 to 12 after the program was added.
Track had the fewest injuries in both years.
Across football, basketball, soccer, and track, injuries fell roughly in half — from 24, 14, 18, and 10 to 12, 7, 9, and 5, respectively.
The program mainly targeted football players at the start.

explanation: The claim is that the drop happened across all sports, not just football. Only choice C cites numbers from every sport in the table, showing the roughly 50% reduction everywhere. A covers only football (the sport the claim explicitly isn't limited to), B is an unrelated ranking, and D is background that doesn't support the all-sports claim.

stem:
An education researcher argued that shorter, more frequent reading practice improves student comprehension more than longer but less frequent sessions of the same total weekly minutes. She assigned four classes to different reading schedules and tested them after eight weeks.

**Reading Comprehension Gains (points on a 100-pt test)**

| Class | Schedule | Total min/week | Mean gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One 60-min session | 60 | 3.1 |
| 2 | Two 30-min sessions | 60 | 4.0 |
| 3 | One 90-min session | 90 | 3.4 |
| 4 | Three 30-min sessions | 90 | 8.7 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's claim?

choices.text: Class 4, which read the most minutes, also had the highest mean gain of 8.7 points.
Every class in the study made at least some gain between the pretest and posttest.

Among classes reading the same total minutes per week, the ones with more frequent sessions gained more: Class 2 (two 30-minute sessions) gained 4.0 points versus Class 1 (one 60-minute session) at 3.1, and Class 4 (three 30-minute sessions) gained 8.7 points versus Class 3 (one 90-minute session) at 3.4.
Classes reading 90 minutes per week averaged larger gains than classes reading only 60 minutes per week.

explanation: The researcher's claim specifically compares schedules of equal total weekly minutes. Only choice C holds the total constant (60 vs. 60; 90 vs. 90) and shows that the more frequent schedule gained more in each pair — the exact controlled comparison the claim requires. A and D compare classes with different totals, and B just says everyone improved.

stem:
An ecologist argued that a beaver dam on Clearwater Creek improved water quality downstream — specifically by lowering sediment and nitrogen levels — while conditions upstream remained essentially unchanged. She gathered measurements before and two years after the dam formed.

**Clearwater Creek Water Quality**

| Location | Metric | 2021 (pre-dam) | 2023 (post-dam) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upstream | Sediment (mg/L) | 48 | 47 |
| Upstream | Nitrogen (mg/L) | 2.0 | 2.1 |
| Downstream | Sediment (mg/L) | 45 | 18 |
| Downstream | Nitrogen (mg/L) | 1.9 | 0.8 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the ecologist's claim?

choices.text: Downstream nitrogen levels were lower than upstream nitrogen levels in 2023.
Sediment levels in the creek declined overall between 2021 and 2023.
After the dam formed, downstream sediment dropped from 45 to 18 mg/L and nitrogen from 1.9 to 0.8 mg/L, while upstream sediment held near 48 mg/L and nitrogen stayed near 2.0 mg/L.
Water quality was measured at both upstream and downstream sites over two years.

explanation: The claim has two halves: downstream improved for both sediment and nitrogen, and upstream didn't. Only choice C backs both — the downstream drops (sediment 45 → 18, nitrogen 1.9 → 0.8) and the nearly flat upstream values (≈48 and ≈2.0) — the contrast that isolates the dam's effect. A compares locations in one year only, B lumps both sites together, and D is methodology.

stem:
A public-health researcher argued that, during the 2024 flu season, the hospital's new triage-by-symptom protocol shortened the average wait time for high-acuity patients without increasing the wait for everyone else. He reviewed emergency department records from two comparable hospitals.

**Average Emergency Department Wait Time (minutes), Flu Season 2024**

| Hospital | Protocol | High-acuity wait | Low-acuity wait | All-patient wait |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mercy General | Standard | 42 | 60 | 55 |
| Saint Luke's | New triage-by-symptom | 18 | 61 | 54 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's claim?

choices.text: Saint Luke's had shorter wait times than Mercy General across every category.
Low-acuity patients at Saint Luke's waited 61 minutes, one minute longer than those at Mercy General.
Both hospitals experienced long wait times during the 2024 flu season.

At Saint Luke's, high-acuity wait times averaged 18 minutes during flu season 2024, less than half of Mercy General's 42 minutes, while Saint Luke's low-acuity (61 vs. 60) and all-patient (54 vs. 55) waits were essentially unchanged from Mercy General's.

explanation: The claim has two parts: high-acuity waits went down, and everyone else's didn't go up. Only choice D pairs the sharp high-acuity improvement (42 → 18) with the near-flat low-acuity (61 vs. 60) and all-patient (54 vs. 55) numbers, supporting both halves at once. A is factually wrong (low-acuity was slightly higher at Saint Luke's), B cites only the small low-acuity detail, and C is vague.

stem:
A climate scientist argued that warming winters are causing sugar maples at lower elevations to produce less sap, while higher-elevation maples are still producing normally. She compared sap yields at four sites in Vermont.

**Average Sap Yield per Tap (liters), 2014 vs. 2024**

| Site | Elevation (m) | 2014 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maple Hollow | 180 | 42 | 28 |
| Birch Bend | 240 | 40 | 30 |
| Cedar Ridge | 520 | 41 | 40 |
| Summit Grove | 610 | 43 | 43 |

Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the scientist's claim?

choices.text: The highest site, Summit Grove, produced 43 liters per tap in both 2014 and 2024.
Every site in the study produced at least 28 liters of sap per tap in 2024.
Sap yields dropped at two of the four sites between 2014 and 2024.

Between 2014 and 2024, sap yield at the low-elevation sites Maple Hollow (42 → 28 L) and Birch Bend (40 → 30 L) fell substantially, while the high-elevation sites Cedar Ridge (41 → 40 L) and Summit Grove (43 → 43 L) barely changed.

explanation: The claim contrasts low-elevation losses with high-elevation stability. Only choice D ties the specific low-elevation drops (42 → 28, 40 → 30) to the near-flat high-elevation values (41 → 40, 43 → 43), supporting both halves of the scientist's claim in one stroke. A covers only the top site, B describes a minimum without any trend, and C notes drops without separating by elevation.
